generated_comment: A: Arre, these days every company wants to show one woman on top, no? Sometimes promotion is less about pure merit and more about image. B: Haan, and if a woman does well also, people say she is very sharp because she thinks like a man. A: Bas wahi, to survive there she has to become so hard only, then naturally people don't find her very approachable.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it undermines women's promotions as driven by diversity optics rather than merit, and frames competence as masculine. It also invokes the double bind by suggesting a successful woman becomes 'hard' and therefore unlikeable or less approachable.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 48. Married for 24 years. A woman who worked for six years before stopping when her first child was born. You are at a family function sitting with three other women your age. One woman mentions that her daughter in law has just gone back to work full time when her child is only eight months old. The conversation opens up. You are giving your opinion on women who return to work this early and what it means for the child and what you think women of this generation have confused about what ambition is supposed to look like and what you gave up and whether you regret it.
